planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant bulbs around 5 inches deep in rich soil with good drainage in full sun. Dark burgundy foliage sets off rosy-pink flower spikes on deep-toned stems. Water and feed through summer, then reduce moisture as the plant goes dormant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; well suited to pots and troughs.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, sharply drained mix. The wavy-edged leaves are heavily spotted maroon and splay out like tentacles. Compact wine-red flower spikes appear low in mid to late summer; keep dry during winter rest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in a bright spot. Foliage and stems carry a strong dark red-purple cast that deepens in full sun. Rosy flower spikes open in mid to late summer above the moody leaves.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in fall in well-drained soil where they can naturalize. Deer and rodents generally leave the bulbs alone. Bright yellow trumpet flowers appear in early to mid spring; let the leaves ripen fully before removing.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Lay tubers about 5 inches deep with the growing eye facing up, and stake tall types at planting. Pinch the growing tips early to encourage branching and more flowers. Blooms come from midsummer to frost; in cold regions dig and store tubers after the first frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in fall in well-drained soil. Deep violet-purple globes sit atop slender stems and draw many pollinators. Bloom comes in late spring; leave the seed heads standing for continued structure.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in fall in gritty soil that stays dry in summer. These little irises are ideal for rock gardens and the front of borders. Jewel-toned blue and purple flowers open in very early spring before most other bulbs.

planting.note: Pot bulbs indoors any time for winter bloom, or plant out in frost-free gardens.

directions: Pot the large bulb with the top third above the soil line and water sparingly until growth begins. Provide bright light and turn the pot for straight stems. Huge trumpet flowers open six to twelve weeks after potting; after bloom, feed the leaves to rebuild the bulb.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late spring once soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Plant tubers about 2 inches deep, knobby side up, only after the soil is reliably warm. Grown for foliage rather than bloom, they need warmth, humidity, and steady moisture. The heart-shaped leaves splashed with pink, red, and white brighten shade all summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in soil that stays moist in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in fall in soil that holds spring moisture but is not waterlogged in summer. This North American native suits meadows and damp borders. Tall spikes of starry blue or white flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn a few inches deep in part shade or sun.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in fall in humus-rich, well-drained soil. Arching stems carry one-sided spikes of silvery white bells striped with green in mid to late spring. It tolerates light shade and naturalizes well under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger has passed.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in spring in full sun and sharp-draining soil. Dense conical spikes of long-lasting white to cream flowers open over several weeks in summer. In cold regions lift and store the bulbs dry over winter, as this South African species is frost tender; the cut stems are famously long-lived in a vase.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring or grow year-round as a potted plant.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in a free-draining mix in a warm, sunny spot. Bright orange, yellow, or occasionally white star flowers form rounded clusters that last for weeks. Often sold as a flowering gift plant, it needs a dry winter rest and protection from frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in mild climates or spring where winters are cold.

directions: Set bulbs about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Fragrant milk-white flowers surround a glossy black central ovary, blooming on sturdy stems in late spring to early summer. It thrives in Mediterranean-climate gardens and makes an excellent cut flower.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant large bulbs four to five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in spring. Tall stems rise to broad flat-topped heads of white flowers with distinctive dark green-black centers in mid to late summer. Lift and store the bulbs frost-free over winter in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for tall late-spring spikes.

directions: Set bulbs about five inches deep in fall in fertile, well-drained soil. Robust upright racemes bear many white star flowers with narrow green stripes in late spring. It is fully hardy and pairs well with alliums and camassias in a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in autumn in free-draining soil. Slender tapering spires open many small white flowers from the base upward in late spring and early summer. This vigorous selection is prized as a durable cut flower and is fully winter hardy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in part shade or woodland edge.

directions: Set bulbs about four inches deep in fall in moist but well-drained ground. Tall slender spikes carry many small greenish-yellow star flowers in early summer. The young flower shoots have long been gathered and cooked like asparagus, giving the plant its common name.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, dry position.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in fall in lean, well-drained soil. Narrow tapering racemes of small white flowers with a fine green midline appear in late spring and early summer. It tolerates dry, sunny sites and naturalizes gently in gravel gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a rock garden or raised bed.

directions: Set the small bulbs two to three inches deep in gritty, sharply drained soil in fall. Low rosettes of hairy-fringed leaves surround short stems of white flowers striped green beneath in early spring. This compact species suits troughs, alpine beds, and the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a well-drained rock garden.

directions: Plant the little bulbs about two inches deep in fall in fast-draining, gritty soil. Broad glossy leaves frame short-stemmed white flowers backed with green in early spring. Sometimes sold under the name balansae, it is ideal for troughs, rock crevices, and the edge of a sunny path.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring for late-summer flower spikes.

directions: Set bulbs five to six inches deep in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Tall stems carry pendant, fragrant white bells reminiscent of a giant snowdrop in mid to late summer. Formerly known as Galtonia candicans, it may be mulched over winter in borderline zones or lifted where the ground freezes hard.

planting.note: Grow as a potted houseplant or plant out only in frost-free gardens.

directions: Sit the large green bulb on top of a gritty potting mix with its base barely buried, since it prefers to grow largely exposed. Numerous bulblets form under the papery skin and can be separated to make new plants. Long arching leaves are topped in time by tall spikes of small green-and-white flowers; keep it warm and on the dry side.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in fall in gritty, free-draining soil. Compact clusters of white star flowers with green-striped backs open in spring above narrow leaves. This low grower naturalizes quietly in rock gardens and sunny meadow plantings.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn while the soil is still workable and moist.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep with the pointed nose up in soil that stays damp through spring. Choose a spot in sun to light shade and leave the clumps undisturbed to naturalize in a meadow or border. Expect spires of star-shaped blue-violet flowers in late spring; let the foliage yellow before removing it.

planting.note: Plant the very large bulbs in fall in a sunny, well-worked bed.

directions: Set the oversized bulbs about 4 inches deep, allowing extra spacing because each one throws a broad rosette of wavy leaves. Grow in full sun with steady spring moisture and average garden soil. Loose spires of pale steel-blue flowers appear in mid to late spring; divide crowded clumps in late summer after the leaves die back.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny prairie or meadow setting.

directions: Set bulbs about 3 to 4 inches deep in deep, seasonally moist soil typical of tallgrass prairie. This species blooms later than most camas, extending the show into early summer. Slender spikes of pale violet stars rise above the grass; allow the plants to set seed to build a naturalized colony.

planting.note: Autumn-plant this southern Oregon native in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 3 to 4 inches deep in ground that is moist in spring and drier in summer. This compact species from southwestern Oregon suits a rock garden or the front of a border. Deep blue-violet flowers open on short spikes in mid to late spring; leave the foliage to ripen fully before tidying.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in deep, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Set the large bulbs 4 to 5 inches deep in rich soil that stays damp through the spring growing season. This blue-flowered subspecies is the wild form behind many garden selections. Tall spires of soft blue-violet stars open in late spring; let clumps settle in for a few years before dividing.

planting.note: Fall-plant bulbs where they can naturalize in moist soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in humus-rich, spring-moist soil in sun or light shade. This selection is prized for its saturated violet-blue racemes that stand well above the foliage. Flowers open in late spring; leave the seedheads for structure or remove them to steer energy back to the bulb.

planting.note: Autumn-plant bulbs in fertile, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Bury bulbs roughly 4 inches deep in rich soil that holds moisture during the spring flush of growth. The creamy-white stars glow in dappled light and pair well with darker-flowered camas. Bloom comes in late spring; allow the leaves to yellow naturally so the bulb can recharge for next year.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in deep, spring-moist soil.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in rich, moisture-holding soil in full sun to light shade. This selection carries fully double, creamy-white flowers that last longer than the single-flowered forms. Bloom appears in late spring; because the double flowers set little seed, propagate by dividing established clumps.

planting.note: Fall-plant this variegated selection in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in rich soil that stays damp in spring, siting them where the foliage can be admired. This selection is grown for its cream-edged variegated leaves as much as its creamy-white flowers. Spikes open in late spring above the striped foliage; divide in late summer to keep the variegation true.

planting.note: Autumn-plant bulbs in a sunny, moisture-retentive border.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep in fertile ground that holds spring moisture yet drains by summer. This selection is chosen for its rich blue racemes on strong, upright stems. Flowers open in late spring and are a magnet for early bees; leave the foliage to ripen before cutting back.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in deep, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Bury the large bulbs 4 to 5 inches deep in rich, spring-moist soil in full sun to light shade. This tall selection carries dense spikes of violet-blue stars that add strong vertical accents to a border. Bloom comes in late spring; give the stems room so they do not lean into neighboring plants.

planting.note: Fall-plant the large bulbs in a sunny, well-prepared bed.

directions: Set the big bulbs about 4 inches deep with generous spacing to accommodate the broad basal leaves. This selection is grown for its deeper, more uniform steel-blue flowers compared with the wild species. Spikes open in mid to late spring; divide crowded clumps after the foliage dies down in summer.

planting.note: Autumn-plant this variegated form in moist, sunny soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in soil that stays reliably moist through spring, where the striped leaves show off. This selection combines cream-edged variegated foliage with deep blue flowers. Spikes open in late spring above the patterned leaves; propagate by division to preserve the variegation.

planting.note: Fall-plant bulbs in a sunny spot with spring moisture.

directions: Set bulbs roughly 4 inches deep in moisture-retentive soil in full sun to light shade. This selection is valued for its intense, deep blue-violet flowers on compact spikes. Bloom arrives in late spring and naturalizes well in a damp meadow; let the leaves die back on their own before mowing or tidying.

planting.note: Autumn-plant bulbs in moist, fertile ground in full sun.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in soil that holds moisture through the spring growing season. This vigorous form, selected from island populations, carries especially deep blue flowers. Spikes open in late spring and naturalize into strong colonies; leave foliage to ripen fully after flowering.

planting.note: Plant the toothlike corms in autumn as soon as they arrive, before they dry out.

directions: Bury the pointed corms three to four inches deep in leafy, well-drained soil, laying them on their side to prevent water pooling in the hollow. The purple-freckled leaves emerge first, followed by solitary pink-lilac flowers with swept-back petals. Do not let the corms bake dry in summer once the foliage disappears.

planting.note: Set corms out in autumn in dappled shade with rich, draining soil.

directions: Bury corms roughly four inches deep in humus-rich woodland soil. Creamy-white flowers with a golden or brownish center are held above heavily mottled leaves in spring. Allow the foliage to yellow and collapse on its own before the summer dormancy sets in.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this vigorous species tolerates ordinary garden borders.

directions: Set corms about four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade. Clear golden-yellow flowers are carried several to a stem above glossy, plain green leaves. This is one of the easier trout lilies to establish and it multiplies quickly into robust clumps.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a bed that dries somewhat during summer dormancy.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil with a dry summer rest. The pale lilac flowers show a distinctive dark purple eye and recurve gracefully above mottled leaves. Water through spring growth, then let the site dry once the foliage yellows.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a cool, moist, sharply drained alpine bed.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in constantly cool, gritty, humus-rich soil that never dries out completely. White flowers with a yellow-orange center open above broad green leaves right at the edge of melting snow. This species is notoriously difficult in lowland gardens and needs a cold, humid microclimate.

planting.note: Plant corms deeply in autumn in cool, rich woodland soil.

directions: Set the corms four to five inches deep in deep, moisture-retentive leaf-mold soil in shade. Rich pinkish-purple flowers with a dark central pattern nod above mottled leaves in spring. Known as katakuri in its homeland, it can be slow to settle but rewards patience with elegant blooms.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a shaded, humus-rich spot.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in rich, well-drained woodland soil. Nodding yellow flowers open above mottled leaves, and the seed capsule sits dimpled against the ground, giving the plant its name. Unlike the yellow trout lily it spreads mainly by seed rather than runners, so leave capsules to ripen.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall; this species tolerates more open, drier ground.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in well-drained soil in a prairie or open-woodland setting. Pale bluish-white flowers open above plain, folded, unmottled leaves in early spring. This species handles drier summers better than most trout lilies once it goes dormant.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in gritty soil with a dry summer rest.

directions: Plant the corms three to four inches deep in sharply drained, gritty soil that dries out in summer. White flowers with a yellow center are borne on branching stems above mottled foliage. Keep the site on the drier side once dormancy begins to avoid rot.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a lightly shaded, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil under high shade. Cream-white flowers flushed with pale lemon at the center rise above marbled leaves. Provide spring moisture followed by a drier summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in draining soil with a warm, dry summer.

directions: Set corms four inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil that bakes somewhat in summer. Fragrant white flowers with a bright yellow center are held above mottled leaves in spring. Water through active growth, then allow a thorough dry rest once the leaves fade.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a cool, gritty, sharply drained alpine bed.

directions: Plant the small corms two to three inches deep in cool, gritty soil in a raised or rock-garden setting. Small white flowers with a yellow center open close to the ground above plain green leaves near melting snow. This is a compact high-elevation species best suited to cool climates.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in gritty mountain soil with steady spring moisture.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil kept moist during spring growth. Several small white flowers per stem age to pink or purple and carry a yellow band at the base. This mountain species prefers cool summers and resents lowland heat.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in cool, moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in cool, leafy soil with reliable moisture. Pale pink to white flowers with a yellow center open above lightly mottled leaves. This is a rare Oregon endemic that appreciates a cool, humid woodland setting.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a lightly shaded, well-drained site.

directions: Set corms about four inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil under high shade. Cream to pale-yellow flowers with an orange central zone rise above faintly mottled leaves. Give spring moisture and a drier summer rest.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in draining, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant the toothlike corms three inches deep in gritty, leafy soil, laying them on their side to shed water. Creamy-white flowers with a dark eye open very early above marbled leaves. This is one of the first Erythronium to bloom, sometimes in late winter in mild gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive garden soil.

directions: Plant corms four to five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Tall stems carry several sulphur-yellow flowers with a faint bronze base above lightly mottled leaves. This robust, vigorous hybrid is among the easiest trout lilies for general garden use and naturalizes into strong clumps.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in humus-rich, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in fertile, well-drained woodland soil in light shade. Creamy-white flowers with a ring of reddish-brown markings at the center rise above handsomely mottled leaves. This is a reliable, free-flowering selection that clumps up steadily over the years.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant corms four to five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in partial shade. Sturdy stems bear several pale sulphur-yellow flowers with a greenish-bronze center above faintly marbled leaves. Like its sibling Pagoda, this is a strong, easy grower that increases well in the garden.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in humus-rich soil that drains freely. Clear lemon-yellow flowers with a subtle reddish ring at the base nod above lightly mottled leaves. This selection flowers freely and holds its color well in dappled woodland light.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in draining, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade. Deep golden-yellow flowers open above green to faintly mottled leaves in mid-spring. This is a compact, cheerful selection well suited to the front of a woodland planting.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in rich, well-drained woodland soil in light shade. Its flowers open a soft yellow and become flushed with pink and apricot tones as they age, giving a two-tone effect above mottled leaves. Leave clumps undisturbed to build up over several seasons.

planting.note: Plant corms promptly in autumn in leafy, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the pointed corms three to four inches deep on their side in humus-rich, draining soil. Reddish-purple flowers with a bronze-green throat and blue tint recurve above mottled leaves. Keep corms from drying out before planting and let the site stay cool through summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in cool, humus-rich, draining soil.

directions: Set the corms three to four inches deep on their side in leafy, well-drained soil. Soft lilac-pink flowers with a darker chocolate-brown base recurve above beautifully mottled foliage. Plant the corms as soon as you receive them to keep them from drying out.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in leafy, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the toothlike corms three to four inches deep on their side in humus-rich, draining soil. Clear rich pink flowers recurve above the characteristic marbled leaves in early spring. Provide dappled shade and keep the ground from baking through summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in cool, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the pointed corms three to four inches deep on their side in leafy, draining soil. Deep purple-pink flowers marked with brown and white at the center recurve above mottled leaves. This is one of the richer-colored selections of the species and shows well in a shaded drift.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in humus-rich, draining soil.

directions: Plant the corms three to four inches deep on their side in cool, leafy soil. Pure white flowers recurve above the mottled foliage, giving a fresh contrast in the spring woodland. Plant promptly and keep the corms plump and unshriveled before setting out.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in leafy, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the pointed corms three to four inches deep on their side in humus-rich, draining soil. Warm rose-pink flowers recurve above marbled leaves in early to mid-spring. Give a cool, lightly shaded spot and avoid letting the ground dry out completely during dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in cool, moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in cool, leaf-mold soil that stays evenly moist in spring. Deep rose-pink flowers with reflexed petals rise above strongly marbled foliage. This selection is a reliable seeder in suitable conditions, so leave capsules to build a colony.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, well-drained garden soil.

directions: Plant corms four to five inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in light shade. Deep golden-yellow flowers are carried above glossy plain green leaves on vigorous stems. This selection shares the easy, clump-forming nature of the species and gives a bold splash of yellow in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade. Clean white flowers with a yellow center are carried above lightly mottled leaves in mid-spring. This is a graceful, free-flowering white selection that settles well into a woodland border.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in cool, humus-rich, draining soil.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in cool, leafy, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Soft pink flowers with recurved petals rise above marbled leaves in spring. This selection raised at a Scottish garden combines good vigor with an elegant pink coloring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in gritty, humus-rich, draining soil.

directions: Plant the corms three inches deep on their side in cool, well-drained, leafy soil. Pink to lilac flowers with a dark central pattern recurve above mottled leaves in early spring. This hardy species from the mountains of Siberia and Central Asia handles hard winters well but dislikes summer wet.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in rich, moist woodland soil.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in light shade. Upward-facing yellow flowers open above mottled leaves, and the seed capsule ends in a distinctive beak. Keep the ground evenly moist through spring growth.

planting.note: Set corms out in autumn so roots establish before winter rains.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep and three inches apart in fast-draining soil where summers stay dry. Leave the grassy foliage to yellow after flowering to recharge the corm, and withhold water once it goes dormant. Loose umbels of violet-blue funnel flowers open on wiry stems in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Autumn planting of dormant corms gives the strongest bloom the following season.

directions: Set the corms three inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed, spacing them a few inches apart for a dense show. This selection is prized for its rich, uniform deep violet-blue and sturdy stems that make excellent cut flowers. Cut or enjoy the star-tipped umbels as they open in early summer, then let the foliage die back naturally.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a spot that bakes dry through summer.

directions: Tuck corms about three inches deep into gritty, sharply draining soil in full sun. The bicolor flowers are white to pale lilac with a bold dark purple-blue stripe down each petal, held in airy umbels. Look for bloom in late spring; allow the leaves to ripen and yellow before tidying them away.

planting.note: Set corms out in autumn in moist-in-winter, dry-in-summer ground.

directions: Plant corms roughly three inches deep and space them a few inches apart in a sunny meadow or border. This species carries flat-topped umbels of milky white, faintly green-veined flowers and tolerates a touch more moisture than most relatives. Blooms arrive in late spring to early summer; let the foliage brown before removing it.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in sharply drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep in gritty soil that dries out in summer. The starry, pale to golden-yellow flowers each carry a fine dark stripe on the petals and open in loose sprays. Expect bloom in early summer, then keep the dormant corms on the dry side.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in a warm, well-drained pocket of the garden.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and lean, free-draining soil. This selection offers clear golden-yellow stars with contrasting dark central stripes on branching stems that suit cutting. Flowers appear in early summer; withhold water once the plant enters summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where the soil stays damp in spring but dry in summer.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep in a spot that tolerates seasonal moisture yet drains freely. The species is distinctive for its very long, spreading flower stalks that hold white to pale blue blooms in an open, airy umbel. Flowering peaks in late spring; let the foliage yellow to feed next year's corm.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in rocky, fast-draining ground in full sun.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil that stays dry through summer. Slender stems carry funnel-shaped, red-purple to violet flowers with flaring lobes in loose clusters. Bloom comes in late spring to early summer; keep the resting corms dry.

planting.note: Set corms out in fall; this species tolerates colder winters than most.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in well-drained soil in an open, sunny site. A hardy, wide-ranging western species, it bears blue to violet trumpet flowers with crimped inner lobes in a loose head. Flowers open in late spring; allow the grassy leaves to die down before summer drought sets in.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in dappled, well-drained woodland edges.

directions: Tuck corms two to three inches deep in loose, quick-draining soil that dries in summer. This subtle species produces greenish to dull yellow flowers with a bronze cast, held in open sprays and often at home in light shade. Bloom appears in late spring; let the corm rest dry after the leaves fade.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Plant corms roughly two inches deep in sharply drained, rocky ground. This compact species carries bright yellow, sometimes blue-tinged flowers in a small umbel on short stems. Blooms open in early summer; withhold water while the plant is dormant.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in lean, fast-draining mountain-type soil.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil in full sun. The species bears pale to golden-yellow flowers with fine dark midveins in an open cluster on wiry stems. Expect flowers in early summer, then keep the resting corm dry.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in a gravelly, sunny, well-drained pocket.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in rocky, sharply draining soil. This dwarf montane species produces yellow, dark-striped flowers in a compact umbel close to the ground, making it a good rock-garden subject. Blooms appear in early to midsummer; allow the small tuft of leaves to ripen fully.

planting.note: Set corms out in autumn in heavy-but-draining soil that dries in summer.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in full sun and soil that bakes dry after spring. Upward-facing violet-purple funnel flowers open in a loose umbel, blooming later than many bulbs as grasses turn gold. Look for flowers in early summer, and leave the foliage to wither before clearing it.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil that stays dry in summer. The short stems carry striking violet-blue flowers with prominent white staminodes at the center. Blooms come in late spring to early summer; keep the dormant corms on the dry side.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in deep, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in a sunny bed with good drainage. The tallest brodiaea, it bears large pink to violet-purple funnel flowers on stout stems well above the leaves. Flowering peaks in early summer; let the foliage yellow and dry to recharge the corm.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a lean, gravelly, sun-baked spot.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in sharply draining, low-fertility soil. This ground-hugging species opens violet flowers almost at soil level on very short stalks, ideal for rock gardens. Bloom appears in late spring; withhold summer water once the leaves fade.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms where the ground is moist in spring and dry in summer.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in soil that holds spring moisture but drains as it dries out. This small species bears violet to purple funnel flowers in a compact cluster on short stems. Blooms open in late spring to early summer; let the plant go fully dormant and dry.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in heavy soil that dries hard in summer.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep in full sun and free-draining ground. Slender, often zig-zagging stems hold pale violet flowers whose staminodes are notably forked at the tips. Flowering comes in early summer; keep the resting corm dry through the dormant months.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in a warm, seasonally moist site with summer drought.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in soil that stays damp in spring and dries in summer. This slender, threadleaf species is a rare southern California native with violet flowers and thin staminodes. Blooms open in late spring; grow it dry once it goes dormant.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in moist-in-spring, dry-in-summer ground.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in seasonally wet, well-draining soil in full sun. This species bears deep blue-violet flowers and, unlike many relatives, lacks the sterile central staminodes. Flowering occurs in early summer; let the corm dry out for its summer rest.

planting.note: Fall-plant corms in a cool, well-drained, part-shaded spot.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in gritty soil in light shade to sun. This dwarf northern California species opens purple, star-shaped flowers with contrasting white staminodes on very short stems. Bloom appears in early summer; keep the small plant dry once dormant.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in heavy clay that drains as it dries in summer.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in full sun and soil that bakes dry in summer. This low species carries violet-purple funnel flowers close to the ground in a tight umbel. Flowers open in late spring to early summer; allow the foliage to yellow before removing it.

planting.note: Plant the large bulbs in autumn while the soil is still workable.

directions: Bury each bulb roughly 4 inches deep in rich, damp soil where it can naturalize into a sturdy clump. This selection is taller and more vigorous than the ordinary species, carrying several green-tipped white bells per stem. Blooms arrive in mid to late spring; divide congested colonies in early summer once the leaves fade.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, retentive soil with some afternoon shade.

directions: Plant bulbs around 3 inches deep in leafy, moisture-holding soil where they can settle undisturbed for years. This Carpathian form is distinguished by yellow-green tips on the petals rather than the usual green. Single bells nod on each stem in late winter to early spring; leave clumps to thicken and lift only when overcrowded.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in cool woodland-type soil.

directions: Set the bulbs roughly 3 inches deep in rich, damp soil in light shade. This robust variety is notable for often producing two flowers on each stem instead of the usual single bell. Green-tipped white flowers open in late winter to early spring; keep the soil from drying during the growing season for the strongest clumps.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in late summer for autumn flowering.

directions: Plant these tiny bulbs about 2 inches deep in gritty, sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny spot. Slender leafless stems carry delicate white bells flushed pink at the base, usually before the thread-like leaves appear. Flowers open in late summer to autumn; allow the bulbs a dry, warm rest once the foliage dies back.

planting.note: Plant in late summer; grow in a pot or frame in colder regions.

directions: Set the small bulbs about 2 inches deep in a very free-draining, gritty mix that dries out in summer. This dainty Corsican species produces soft pink bells on fine stems, appearing with or just before the narrow leaves. Flowers open in autumn; protect from winter wet and hard frost, using a container or bulb frame where it is not reliably hardy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early autumn; keep frost-free in cold climates.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 2 inches deep in sharply drained, sunny soil that stays dry through summer dormancy. This rare species from the hills near Nice bears small nodding white bells above grassy leaves. Flowers appear in early spring; it resents winter wet, so a bulb frame or alpine house suits it best outside mild regions.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in late summer for autumn bloom.

directions: Bury the bulbs about 2 inches deep in gritty, fast-draining soil in a warm, sheltered position. This Spanish species carries small white bells on slim stems, the flowers opening before or with the emerging leaves. Blooms appear in autumn; give it a dry summer rest and shelter from cold, wet winters.

planting.note: Plant in early autumn; needs a dry summer dormancy.

directions: Plant bulbs about 2 inches deep in very free-draining, sandy soil that bakes dry in summer. Named for its slender, thread-fine leaves, this Iberian and North African species carries one to three white bells, sometimes pink-tinged, per stem. Flowers open in late winter to spring; keep it frost-protected and completely dry while dormant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn; best in a pot or alpine house in cold areas.

directions: Set the small bulbs roughly 2 inches deep in gritty, sharply drained compost in full sun. This Corsican endemic produces tiny white bells above narrow leaves and stays compact. Flowers open in spring; it needs a warm, dry summer rest and protection from winter wet, so many growers keep it under glass.

planting.note: Tuck bulbs into a sunny bed or rock garden in early autumn.

directions: Set the bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in free-draining soil during fall, spacing them a few inches apart in generous drifts. This is the most common named form, prized for its soft lilac-blue stars over slim grassy leaves. Blooms open in early spring and the clumps thicken quickly by offset, so lift and divide crowded patches every few years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn where the large white flowers can be admired up close.

directions: Plant the bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in well-drained soil in fall, giving this vigorous selection a little extra elbow room. Named for the Argentine plantsman who introduced it, it carries the largest, purest-white flowers of the group on slightly taller stems. Flowers appear in early to mid spring and the plant bulks up steadily, making it excellent for naturalizing.

planting.note: Set bulbs in a warm, sheltered spot in autumn for the brightest blue display.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 2 inches deep in sharply drained soil in fall, choosing a warm border or the front of a rock garden. This selection stands out for its intense, almost sky-blue rounded flowers held on compact stems above darker green leaves. It is slightly more frost-tender than the typical species, so mulch lightly in colder gardens and enjoy the vivid spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a spot where the deep violet color reads well.

directions: Set the bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in well-drained soil during autumn, massing them for the strongest effect. This is the darkest of the common selections, opening dusky violet-purple stars that contrast nicely with paler blue forms nearby. Bloom comes in early to mid spring; leave the foliage to yellow naturally so the bulbs recharge for next year.

planting.note: Plant the bulbs in autumn near a path where the pink flowers can be seen.

directions: Plant the bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in fall. This unusual pink-flowered form has soft rosy-mauve stars, a rare color break among the mostly blue and white kinds. It flowers in early spring and clumps up slowly, so give it a sheltered, well-drained pocket and divide only when congested.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in autumn in full sun for the richest blue tone.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 2 to 3 inches deep in well-drained soil in fall, grouping them for impact. This selection is valued for its deep, saturated blue flowers that hold their color well in the spring garden. Blooms appear in early to mid spring above the usual grassy leaves; leave clumps to naturalize and thin only when they become crowded.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for a clean white spring carpet.

directions: Set the bulbs 2 to 3 inches deep and a few inches apart in free-draining soil during fall. This is the pure white form of the species, its faintly starry flowers sometimes carrying only a whisper of blue in the veins. It blooms in early spring and naturalizes readily, so plant it in broad drifts under deciduous shrubs where it can spread.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a warm, very well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 2 inches deep in sharply drained, gritty soil in autumn. This yellow-flowered species is lower and more spreading than the blue kinds, opening bright golden stars close to the ground. It appreciates warmth and dislikes winter wet, so grow it in a raised bed or trough and expect flowers in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a sheltered rock garden or alpine trough.

directions: Set the bulbs about 2 inches deep in a lean, fast-draining mix in autumn, ideally in a protected pocket or container. This dwarf species carries clear yellow, wide-open stars on very short stems and is happiest with a dry summer rest. It is more cold-tender than the common blue starflowers, so overwinter it in a cold frame or alpine house in colder climates; look for flowers in early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sheltered, sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Plant the bulbs about 2 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in fall, choosing a warm and sheltered spot. This species is distinguished by its curled, recurving grassy leaves and small white flowers that often open in late winter, earlier than most kin. It resents cold wet soil, so give it excellent drainage and let the foliage die back naturally after the early bloom.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits Mediterranean climates; lift or plant in spring in colder zones.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in a gritty, free-draining bed that bakes dry in summer. Keep the soil evenly moist while leaves are green and withhold water after bloom to enforce dormancy. Solitary large purple-to-cream flowers open on slender stems in spring.

planting.note: Best planted in fall in frost-free gardens; treat as a spring-planted annual elsewhere.

directions: Tuck corms two inches into sandy soil and give full sun with excellent drainage. Water through the growing months and allow the plant to dry out completely once flowering ends. Creamy-yellow to white blooms appear in spring, often self-seeding where happy.

planting.note: Formerly Streptanthera elegans; plant corms in autumn in mild regions.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in lean, gritty soil in a sunny spot. Provide steady moisture during winter and spring growth, then keep bone dry through the summer rest. Flat, salmon-to-orange flowers with a dark ringed center open in spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in dry-summer climates for spring bloom.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in sandy, quick-draining soil and site in full sun. Water while foliage is active and cut off irrigation as leaves fade for the summer. Soft yellow, lightly scented flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in frost-free gardens; spring-plant in colder areas.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in gritty soil with sharp drainage and full exposure. Keep evenly moist during growth and dry during dormancy to prevent rot. Pink to rose flowers with pale centers rise on wiry stems in spring.

planting.note: Autumn planting in mild climates gives the strongest spring display.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun. Water through the cool season and let the bed dry out after flowering. Compact stems carry pale pink to mauve flowers with yellow throats in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where winters stay mild and summers run dry.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in lean, gritty soil that drains freely. Maintain moisture during the growing months and enforce a dry summer rest. Small purple flowers with dark markings open low to the ground in spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in dry-summer climates for a spring show.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in sandy soil with excellent drainage and full sun. Water regularly while green and stop entirely once leaves yellow. Yellow-and-purple patterned flowers appear on upright stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild gardens; treat as spring-planted where cold.

directions: Tuck corms two inches into gritty, quick-draining soil in full sun. Keep evenly watered through growth and dry through summer dormancy. Numerous small pink to purple flowers open along slender stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild regions, or early spring where winters freeze.

directions: Set corms three inches deep and a few inches apart in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Water through the cool growing season and keep dry after the foliage dies back. Cupped orange-to-yellow flowers with a dark central blotch open on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in frost-free gardens; protect from any freeze.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sharply drained, sandy soil in full sun. Water steadily while in leaf and withhold water once flowering finishes for a dry rest. Tall spikes of striking turquoise-green flowers with a black-and-purple eye appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for tall spring spikes in mild climates.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. Give consistent moisture through the growing season and dry conditions in summer. Long-tubed creamy flowers flushed pink open atop tall, slender stems in spring.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits Mediterranean and coastal gardens.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sandy soil with excellent drainage and full sun. Water through the cool season, then hold it dry after the leaves fade. Bell-shaped red to pink flowers open along the stem in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where winters stay mild.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep in lean, quick-draining soil in full sun. Keep watered while green and let the bed dry completely in summer. Variable flowers in blue, pink, or white with a contrasting center open in spring.

planting.note: Fall-plant in dry-summer climates for spring bloom.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun. Water regularly during growth and stop once the foliage yellows. Bright orange-yellow flowers with a dark eye open on slim stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild-winter regions.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. Provide moisture through the cool season and enforce a dry summer dormancy. Branching spikes of white to pale mauve flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in mild climates; spring-plant where cold.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Water while foliage is active and withhold it after bloom. Dainty pink to white flowers open along zigzagging stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for spring flowering in frost-free gardens.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained, sandy soil in full sun. Keep evenly moist through growth and dry through the summer rest. Slender spikes of pale blue-lilac flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in dry-summer regions for a spring display.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in lean, quick-draining soil in full sun. Water during the growing months and keep dry once leaves fade. Bright yellow flowers with a darker center open on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild gardens, or early spring where winters freeze.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil in a hot, sunny site. Water through the cool growing season and stop after the foliage dies down. White petals backed with violet-blue and a dark eye open along the spike in late spring.

planting.note: Autumn planting gives strong spring bloom in frost-free climates.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. Keep moist while in growth and enforce a dry summer rest. Soft rose-pink flowers with a deeper center open on tall wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild regions for spring flowers.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun. Water steadily through the growing season and dry off after flowering. Creamy-white flowers with a dark maroon center open along slender spikes in late spring.

planting.note: Autumn-plant in frost-free gardens; spring-plant where cold.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in quick-draining, sandy soil in a sunny bed. Water through the cool season and keep dry through the summer dormancy. Clear golden-yellow flowers open along the spike in late spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild-winter gardens for spring bloom.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Provide moisture during active growth and withhold it after the leaves fade. Deep carmine-pink flowers with a darker throat open on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: In winter-rainfall climates set corms in autumn; in colder zones plant after the last frost.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil that stays dry through summer dormancy. Water regularly while leaves are growing and taper off once the tall pink spikes fade. Expect the branched flower stems in late spring; lift and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Set corms in autumn where winters are mild; this vigorous form also spreads by stem bulbils.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun and give room, as this form multiplies quickly. Snap off the small bulbils along the stems if you want to limit self-sowing, since they root readily. Brick-orange flowers open on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits mild winters; delay to after frost in colder gardens.

directions: Bury corms three to four inches deep in free-draining ground and water through the growing months. Let the soil dry as the strappy leaves yellow after bloom. Salmon-to-orange tubular flowers rise on branched spikes in late spring.

planting.note: One of the hardier species; plant corms in spring in cold zones or autumn where mild.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and average, well-drained soil. This summer-growing species tolerates a little more moisture and cold than most watsonias, so mulch the crown in borderline zones. Orange-red flower spikes appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Autumn-planted corms bloom the following spring in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil kept dry over summer. This compact species carries long, drooping tubular flowers that look like a small aloe. Coral-red blooms open in spring, earlier than most watsonias.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in winter-rainfall climates; the blue-green leaves stand out even before bloom.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun with excellent drainage. The broad, glaucous, red-edged leaves are handsome, and the tall spikes of soft pink flowers carry a light fragrance. Blooms rise in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: A small species suited to autumn planting in mild winters or containers elsewhere.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in a gritty, fast-draining mix. This is one of the shorter watsonias, good for the front of a dry bed or a pot. Short spikes of orange, salmon, or purple flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: A summer-growing mountain species; plant corms in spring.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in full sun and well-drained soil. Water during the summer growing season and reduce it in winter dormancy. Tall spikes of orange to pink flowers open in summer.

planting.note: A summer-rainfall species from eastern South Africa; plant corms in spring and water through summer.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun with steady summer moisture. The flowers are packed densely on the upper spike, giving a bold column of color. Bright pink to mauve blooms appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: A dwarf winter grower; plant corms in autumn where winters stay mild and moist.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in a sharply drained, seasonally moist spot. This is among the smallest and most brilliantly colored watsonias, ideal for a pot or rock ledge. Vivid scarlet flowers open low on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Winter-growing from the Cape Peninsula; plant corms in autumn in mild climates.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun and gritty soil that dries out in summer. This robust species makes tall, stately spikes above sturdy leaves. Salmon-pink flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; this species grows through the warmer months.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in full sun and well-drained soil. Keep it watered through the growing season and drier when dormant. Pale pink flowers open on tall branched spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Naturally grows near water, so it accepts more moisture than most; plant corms in spring.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in a spot that stays reliably moist through summer. Unlike drier-loving watsonias, this one tolerates damp ground and even streambanks. Red to orange-red flowers rise on tall spikes in summer.

planting.note: A shorter winter-growing species; plant corms in autumn in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in seasonally moist, well-drained soil. This compact species suits the front of a border or a container. Pink flowers with a lighter throat open on short spikes in spring.

planting.note: A mountain species that grows in the warmer months; plant corms in spring.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil. Water while in active growth and keep drier during dormancy. Orange to red flowers appear on slender spikes in summer.

planting.note: A summer-growing species; plant corms in spring and water through the warm season.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in full sun and free-draining ground. Provide summer water while it grows and let it rest dry in winter. Coral to orange flowers open on upright spikes in summer.

planting.note: A winter-growing Cape species; plant corms in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil kept dry in summer. The relatively large flowers give the plant its name. Bright red to pink blooms open on arching spikes in spring.

planting.note: A tall winter grower; plant corms in autumn in mild-winter climates.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil. Give room for the tall flowering stems that need dry summer conditions to ripen the corms. Pink to white flowers open on stately spikes in late spring.

planting.note: A summer-growing mountain species from the Kogelberg; plant corms in spring.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in full sun and very well-drained soil. Water during the growing season and keep it drier at rest. Bright orange-red flowers open on upright spikes in summer.

planting.note: A tall winter-growing species; plant corms in autumn where winters stay mild.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun with excellent drainage and a dry summer rest. Give the tall stems space and shelter from strong wind. Orange to red flowers rise on branched spikes in late spring.

planting.note: A pure-white selection of the Cape bugle-lily; grow from corms since seedlings do not come true.

directions: Plant corms four inches deep in full sun and gritty soil that dries through summer. Because this white form is grown vegetatively, propagate by lifting and separating cormlets rather than seed. Tall spikes of clean white flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: A compact garden hybrid; plant corms in spring in cold zones or autumn where mild.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and well-drained soil. This shorter hybrid suits mixed borders and needs staking less often than the wild species. Soft pink flowers open on neat spikes in summer; divide clumps to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: A winter-growing species; plant corms in autumn where winters are mild and moist.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. This little species is prized for flowers with a red center ringed by deep blue. Blooms open low to the ground in early spring; keep the soil dry over the summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild-winter climates; the leaves are notably hairy.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and gritty soil. Water through the growing season and allow a dry rest once the leaves die back. Deep red to crimson flowers with dark anthers open in spring.

planting.note: A small winter grower; plant corms in autumn where frosts are light.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in fast-draining, seasonally moist soil. Give full sun and a dry summer to ripen the corms. Blue to violet flowers with pale markings open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates to enjoy the scented spring flowers.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and free-draining soil. Water while the pleated leaves grow and let them dry after bloom. Sweetly scented cream to yellow flowers open in spring.

planting.note: One of the smallest baboon flowers; plant corms in autumn in mild-winter gardens or pots.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Its small size makes it ideal for a container or trough. Fragrant violet to mauve flowers open close to the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn where winters are mild; the flowers are noticeably fragrant.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in full sun and sharply drained soil. Keep it watered during winter growth and dry through summer dormancy. Scented blue to purple flowers open in spring.

planting.note: A distinctive bird-pollinated species; plant corms in autumn in mild climates.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and gritty soil. This species produces a bare, stiff perching stem that sunbirds cling to while feeding. Bright red flowers cluster near the base in spring; keep it dry once dormant.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates; the long-tubed flowers are often scented at night.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in fast-draining soil in full sun. Water through winter growth and withhold it after flowering. Cream to pale flowers with a long floral tube open in spring, often fragrant toward evening.

planting.note: A low-growing species with large flowers; plant corms in autumn where mild.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil in full sun. Despite its small stature it carries relatively large, showy flowers. Yellow blooms with a dark central blotch open near ground level in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild-winter climates; leaves are arranged in a flat fan.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and well-drained soil. Water during the growing months and allow a dry summer rest. Fragrant blue to lilac flowers open on short spikes in spring.

planting.note: A small fragrant species; plant corms in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. Keep it moist during winter growth and dry through dormancy. Fragrant blue to violet flowers with pale central marks open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates for spring color.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Water through the cool season and let the corms dry off in summer. Blue to purple flowers open on short spikes in spring.

planting.note: A winter-growing species; plant corms in autumn where frosts are light.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in full sun and fast-draining soil. Provide winter moisture and a dry summer dormancy. Pale to greenish flowers open on short stems in spring.

planting.note: A sandveld species; plant corms in autumn in mild-winter climates.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Water while it grows in the cool months and keep it dry once dormant. Blue to violet flowers with contrasting markings open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in mild climates; the leaves have a rough texture.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in gritty soil in full sun. Give winter water during active growth and a dry summer rest. Fragrant pale blue to mauve flowers open on short spikes in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a spot where the pure flowers show against dark soil or foliage.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in gritty, free-draining ground in late summer. The clean white chalices open in early autumn ahead of any foliage. Spring leaves nourish the corm before yellowing, so let them wither naturally.

planting.note: Plant in late summer where the many-petaled blooms can be admired closely.

directions: Bury corms about three inches deep in sharply drained soil during August. Fully double white flowers packed with petals appear in autumn and benefit from a sheltered spot since the heavy heads can flop after rain. Allow spring leaves to feed the corm before removing them.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer among low groundcover that supports the flowers.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in late summer. The lilac-pink double flowers open in autumn and last longer sheltered from wind. Let the spring foliage die down on its own to build next year's bloom.

planting.note: Plant the large corms in late summer for robust autumn flowers.

directions: Plant the plump corms four inches deep in rich, well-drained soil during late summer. Large weatherproof goblets in rosy purple with a white throat appear in autumn on sturdy stems. Big glossy leaves emerge in spring and should be left until they yellow.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer where the substantial white flowers make a focal point.

directions: Set the large corms about four inches deep in humus-rich, free-draining soil in late summer. Broad rounded goblets of glistening pure white rise in autumn and stand up well to weather. Spring leaves are large and lush, so give the clump room and let them fade naturally.

planting.note: Plant in late summer with low companions to prop up the heavy double blooms.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This famous fully double variety opens many-petaled lilac-pink flowers resembling water lilies in autumn, which tend to sprawl and appreciate the support of surrounding groundcover. Let the vigorous spring foliage die back before tidying.

planting.note: Plant the big corms in late summer for large, early autumn flowers.

directions: Set the sizable corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Large violet-mauve goblets with white centers open freely in early autumn. The broad spring leaves are substantial, so allow generous spacing and leave the foliage until it withers.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for a reliable, long-flowering autumn display.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in well-drained ground during late summer. Numerous rosy-lilac chalices with faint white throats open over several weeks in autumn. Let the spring leaves feed the corm and remove them only after they yellow.

planting.note: Plant the large corms in late summer for an abundant early autumn show.

directions: Set the broad corms three to four inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. A single corm can throw many soft lilac-pink flowers, making it one of the earliest and most generous autumn bloomers. Large ribbed spring leaves follow and should be left to die down naturally.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer where the crisp white flowers stand out.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in late summer. Slender white flowers appear early in autumn ahead of the foliage. Allow the ribbed spring leaves to mature fully before they wither so the corm stays vigorous.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in sharply drained soil during late summer. Clusters of honey-scented rosy-purple flowers open in mid to late autumn, often many per corm. Give it a sunny, sheltered spot and let the broad spring leaves die back on their own.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, free-draining position.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This selection carries deeper purple-toned flowers in autumn clusters. Site it in full sun for the richest color, and leave the spring foliage until it fades.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a sunny, gritty spot such as a raised bed.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in very free-draining, sunny soil in late summer. The pointed pinkish-purple petals carry a striking chequered tessellation and appear in early autumn. This smaller species prefers a warm, dryish site and resents heavy, wet ground.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, sharply drained position.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in gritty, sun-baked soil in late summer. Its goblets show a bold purple-and-white chequerboard pattern in autumn. Give it the warmest, best-drained spot you have and keep it on the dry side during summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant the small corms in late summer in a rock garden or trough.

directions: Set the small corms about two inches deep in very gritty, free-draining soil in late summer. Dainty pinkish flowers open in autumn as narrow leaves emerge alongside them, unlike most colchicums. This diminutive species suits an alpine trough or sharp-draining rockery.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer where the very large spring leaves have room.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. Large tessellated pink flowers open in autumn, followed in spring by exceptionally broad pleated leaves that need generous space. Allow the big foliage to die down completely before removing it.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, very well-drained site.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in gritty, sun-warmed soil in late summer. Strongly chequered pinkish-purple flowers with pointed petals appear in autumn. This choice species needs excellent drainage and a dry summer rest, making it ideal for a bulb frame.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for large early flowers.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Large rosy-lilac goblets with paler throats and contrasting purple anthers open early in autumn. Broad leaves appear in spring and should be allowed to yellow before removal.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. Slender pinkish-purple flowers open in autumn on a reliable, easy-to-grow species. Let the spring foliage mature and fade before tidying the clump.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, sharply drained site.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil in late summer. Small pale pink to white flowers appear from autumn into early winter in mild climates. This Mediterranean species needs a warm, dry summer rest and excellent drainage.

planting.note: Plant the small corms in late summer in a rock garden or raised bed.

directions: Set the small corms about two inches deep in very free-draining soil in late summer. Delicate rosy-pink flowers open in autumn on this compact species. It thrives in a sunny alpine bed with a warm, dry dormant period.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in gritty soil in late summer. Faintly chequered pinkish-purple flowers open from early to mid autumn. It appreciates a hot, dry summer rest and sharp drainage.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer in an open, well-drained site.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. Bright pinkish-purple flowers appear in autumn on this robust central European species. Allow the spring leaves to die down naturally to fuel next season's bloom.

planting.note: Plant corms in early autumn for late winter or very early spring flowers.

directions: Plant the small corms about two inches deep in very gritty, well-drained soil in early autumn. Unlike autumn bloomers, this species flowers in late winter to early spring with small white to pale pink blooms with dark anthers. Give it a sunny rock garden or trough and a dry summer rest.
